does: This paper introduces Fed-FBD, a federated learning architecture that decomposes ResNet models into six functional blocks (stem, four residual groups, classification head) and maintains a warehouse of N independently tracked color variants assembled from contributor-stamped blocks. The system enables three core capabilities: architecturally guaranteed block-level isolation that prevents adversarial clients from contaminating clean variants, privacy-by-design where membership inference provides no advantage over chance without additional mechanisms, and surgical machine unlearning that removes a departed participant's contribution in sub-second time. The approach addresses fundamental limitations of FedAvg by moving beyond treating clients as black boxes.
connects: This work builds on the federated learning framework established by FedAvg and other distributed training methods, extending it to handle adversarial robustness, privacy, and unlearning—three critical problems in collaborative learning with sensitive data. It responds to growing concerns about isolation of misbehaving contributors, auditing per-client influence, and fulfilling data deletion requests (the right to be forgotten), which are poorly addressed by standard aggregation schemes. The paper combines ideas from modular neural network design, privacy-by-design principles from differential privacy literature, and machine unlearning research, creating a system where these concerns are built into the architecture rather than layered on top.
enables: Fed-FBD opens pathways for practical federated learning in regulated domains like healthcare where privacy guarantees, auditability, and deletion rights are mandatory. The block-level isolation mechanism enables more trustworthy multi-institutional collaborations by allowing clients to detect and exclude adversarial participants without global model retraining. The surgical unlearning capability supports compliance with GDPR and similar frameworks at computational cost compatible with real deployments. Future work could extend this architecture to other model families, optimize the color warehouse approach for memory efficiency, formalize the privacy guarantees under composition, and explore how to handle poisoning attacks that operate within individual blocks rather than across them.
